tech.headline: Oxford PV hits 25.6% efficiency on shingled tandem module

tech.headline: China develops heavy‑liquid recycling for silicon modules
tech.summary: Chinese researchers created a three‑step recycling process for end‑of‑life crystalline silicon PV modules. The method recovers high‑purity silicon, copper, silver compounds, and other valuable materials while allowing reagent reuse. Life‑cycle analysis shows an 80.42% carbon‑emission reduction versus conventional methods and positive returns from solar‑cell and solder‑strip stages.

learn.title: Second Life EV Batteries for Grid Storage
learn.intro: As electric vehicles retire, their large lithium‑ion packs still hold 70‑80% capacity. Repurposing these batteries for stationary storage can cut grid costs, smooth renewable output, and avoid waste, making the clean‑energy transition faster and cheaper.
learn.c1_text: A used EV pack is removed after ~8‑10 years and tested. Even at 70% capacity it can store 10‑15 kWh, enough to power a typical home for a day—similar to a refurbished laptop battery that still runs a notebook well.
learn.c2_text: When stacked, dozens of second‑life modules act like a modular battery bank, delivering fast response for frequency regulation and peak‑shaving. Their lower upfront cost (≈30% cheaper than new storage) makes them attractive for utilities.
learn.c3_text: Delhi Metro and Karnataka Power Corp. have launched pilots using retired Tata Nexon EV batteries to support solar‑plus‑storage micro‑grids, aiming to add 200 MWh of second‑life capacity by 2026.
learn.stat1: Global EV stock reached 16 million units in 2023
learn.stat3: IRENA estimates second‑life storage could cut storage costs by 20‑30%
learn.stat4: India targets 5 GW of stationary storage by 2030

learn.title: Geothermal Energy Basics and Uses
learn.intro: Geothermal taps the Earth’s natural heat, providing reliable baseload power and heating without fuel combustion. It offers a low‑carbon alternative for both electricity and district heating, crucial for decarbonising industry and cities.
learn.c1_title: 🌍 Where does geothermal heat come from?
learn.c1_text: Heat originates from radioactive decay in the crust and residual planetary formation energy. At depths of 3‑5 km, temperatures can exceed 150 °C—enough to boil water and spin turbines, similar to a giant natural kettle.
learn.c2_text: Fluid circulates through hot rock, absorbs heat, and returns to the surface to drive a turbine. Binary cycle plants use a secondary fluid with a lower boiling point, allowing electricity generation from lower‑temperature resources.
learn.c3_text: The Deccan Traps host > 10 GW technical potential. The Ministry of New & Renewable Energy supports pilot projects in Rajasthan and Gujarat, aiming for 1 GW installed by 2030.
learn.stat3: US DOE reports binary plants can operate at 5‑10 °C lower temps
learn.stat4: India’s geothermal resource map shows > 5,000 MW in Gujarat alone

learn.title: Pumped Hydro – The World’s Largest Battery
learn.intro: Pumped hydro storage (PHS) moves water between two reservoirs to store energy, providing gigawatt‑scale capacity and multi‑hour to multi‑day discharge—key for balancing high shares of wind and solar.
learn.c1_text: During excess renewable generation, water is pumped uphill to a higher reservoir. When power is needed, it flows back down through turbines, similar to a giant reversible waterwheel.
learn.c2_text: PHS can ramp from 0 to full power in seconds and operate for up to 24 hours continuously, delivering up to 8 GW of power in some sites—far beyond any battery chemistry.
learn.c3_text: India plans 10 GW of new PHS by 2030, with projects in Himachal Pradesh and Karnataka. These installations will support the nation’s target of 450 GW renewable capacity by 2030.
learn.stat3: IRENA notes PHS provides 45% of world’s storage capacity

learn.title: India’s National Green Hydrogen Mission
learn.intro: The mission aims to make India a global hub for clean hydrogen, reducing carbon emissions in steel, fertilizer and transport. Strong policy incentives are designed to accelerate production, export and domestic use by 2030.
learn.c1_text: Produce 5 million tonnes of green hydrogen per year by 2030, with 10 GW of electrolyzer capacity by 2025. This is akin to adding the output of 50 large coal plants, but without CO₂.
learn.c2_text: The government offers a 3.5 % capital subsidy, tax holidays for 10 years, and a green hydrogen credit of ₹1.5 kWh⁻¹. These measures lower the levelized cost of hydrogen by ~30 %.
learn.c3_text: Gujarat’s 1 GW electrolyzer at Gujarat Energy Research Institute and a 2 GW pilot in Odisha are slated for 2025, backed by state‑level renewable PPAs and water‑security guarantees.
learn.stat3: Projected green hydrogen export value: $2 billion by 2030

learn.title: Solar Panel Types: Mono, Poly & Thin‑Film
learn.intro: Choosing the right solar technology affects efficiency, cost and suitability for rooftops or large farms. Understanding the trade‑offs helps stakeholders design optimal solar solutions for diverse Indian climates.
learn.c1_text: Made from a single silicon crystal, they achieve 20‑22% efficiency—like a high‑grade chocolate bar delivering more energy per bite. They perform best in limited space, common on urban rooftops.
learn.c2_text: Produced from multiple silicon crystals, they reach 15‑18% efficiency. Costs are 10‑15% lower than mono, making them popular for utility‑scale farms where land is abundant.
learn.c3_text: Cadmium‑telluride (CdTe) and CIGS thin‑film modules deliver 12‑14% efficiency but perform better in high‑temperature, low‑light conditions—useful for Rajasthan’s hot deserts where conventional panels lose output.
learn.stat3: Average LCOE for utility‑scale polycrystalline: $0.028 kWh⁻¹
learn.stat4: Thin‑film projects in India total 0.8 GW (2024)

learn.title: Renewable Purchase Obligation for Indian DISCOMs
learn.intro: RPO mandates that power distribution companies (DISCOMs) buy a set percentage of electricity from renewable sources, driving demand, stabilising markets and accelerating India's clean‑energy targets.
learn.c1_text: Each DISCOM must source a minimum share—currently 20% for solar and 10% for wind—of its total annual purchase. Failure incurs penalties of up to ₹0.5 kWh⁻¹, incentivising contracts with renewable generators.
learn.c2_text: DISCOMs meet RPO through Power Purchase Agreements, Renewable Energy Certificates (RECs) or by investing in their own renewable assets. RECs trade on exchanges, acting like carbon credits for electricity.
learn.c3_text: By FY2024‑25, DISCOMs collectively procured 45 GW of renewable power, up from 30 GW in 2020, helping India achieve its 450 GW renewable goal for 2030.
learn.stat1: Current solar RPO target: 20% of DISCOM supply
learn.stat2: Wind RPO target: 10% of DISCOM supply
learn.stat3: REC market turnover: 12 million REC units (2023)
